question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral / isHealthy] Can an unprivileged borrower, by calling supplyCollateral with assets=0 when position.collateral[collateralIndex] is already zero, leave collateralBitmap with bit i unset while collateral[i] remains zero, and then later supply a nonzero amount to the same index without triggering the setBit guard, under preconditions where the borrower has existing debt and the market has multiple collateral slots, via the sequence supplyCollateral(index, 0) → supplyCollateral(index, X) where X > 0 and oldCollateral is now X not zero, violating the invariant that collateralBitmap bit i is set if and only if collateral[i] > 0, causing scoped impact: isHealthy loop skips collateral[i] in maxDebt computation, making a healthy position appear undercollateralized and liquidatable? Proof idea: fuzz (collateralIndex, assets_first, assets_second) and assert collateralBitmap bit i is set iff collateral[i] > 0 after each supplyCollateral call.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: withdrawCollateral / isHealthy] Can an unprivileged borrower call withdrawCollateral with assets=0 when collateral[collateralIndex] is already zero and the bit is set (phantom bit), under preconditions where a prior sequence left the bit set without corresponding collateral, via the sequence supplyCollateral(i, X) → withdrawCollateral(i, X) → withdrawCollateral(i, 0), violating the invariant that clearBit is only called when newCollateral==0 && assets>0, causing scoped impact: phantom bit in collateralBitmap causes isHealthy and liquidate loops to call IOracle(collateralParam.oracle).price() for a zero-amount collateral, and if that oracle reverts, the position is permanently frozen? Proof idea: unit test the exact condition newCollateral==0 && assets==0 and assert clearBit is not called, then verify oracle is still called in the loop.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / isHealthy] Can an unprivileged liquidator pass a collateralIndex that is not set in position.collateralBitmap but for which position.collateral[collateralIndex] > 0 (due to a prior bitmap desync), under preconditions where the bitmap desync exists and the position is unhealthy, via the sequence that causes desync → liquidate(collateralIndex=i, seizedAssets>0, repaidUnits=0), causing liquidatedCollatPrice to remain 0 (since i is never matched in the bitmap loop at Midnight.sol:611), so repaidUnits = seizedAssets.mulDivUp(0, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif) = 0, violating the invariant that seized collateral must correspond to nonzero repaid debt, causing scoped impact: liquidator seizes collateral for zero repaid units, draining borrower collateral without reducing debt? Proof idea: construct a state where collateral[i]>0 but bit i is unset, then call liquidate with seizedAssets>0 and assert repaidUnits==0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate (onLiquidate callback)] Can an unprivileged liquidator's onLiquidate callback reenter supplyCollateral on behalf of the borrower (if authorized) to set a new bitmap bit after the liquidation loop has already computed maxDebt and badDebt from the pre-callback bitmap, under preconditions where the liquidator is authorized for the borrower and the market has a liquidatorGate that allows the liquidator, via the sequence liquidate() → onLiquidate callback → supplyCollateral(borrower, newIndex, largeAmount) → callback returns → debt reduced by repaidUnits, violating the invariant that maxDebt and badDebt must reflect all active collateral at the time of liquidation, causing scoped impact: bad debt is over-realized (borrower's new collateral not counted), slashing lenders more than warranted? Proof idea: stateful fuzz with a reentrant onLiquidate callback that supplies collateral and assert badDebt <= actual undercollateralization.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate (onLiquidate callback)] Can an unprivileged liquidator's onLiquidate callback reenter withdrawCollateral on behalf of the borrower (if authorized) to clear a bitmap bit after the liquidation loop has already computed maxDebt using that collateral, under preconditions where the liquidator is authorized for the borrower and the position has multiple active collaterals, via the sequence liquidate() → onLiquidate callback → withdrawCollateral(borrower, otherIndex, allCollateral) → callback returns → debt reduced, violating the invariant that the collateral counted in maxDebt must still be present after liquidation, causing scoped impact: borrower's remaining position is undercollateralized after liquidation because collateral was withdrawn during the callback, leaving bad debt unrealized? Proof idea: unit test with reentrant callback that withdraws collateral and assert isHealthy(borrower) after liquidation.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy / take] Can an unprivileged seller (borrower) whose collateralBitmap has a phantom bit (bit set, collateral[i]=0) for a collateral whose oracle always reverts, under preconditions where the phantom bit was introduced by a prior sequence and the seller has nonzero debt, via the sequence take(offer where seller=borrower) → isHealthy(market, id, seller) → loop calls IOracle(collateralParam.oracle).price() for phantom index → oracle reverts, violating the invariant that a healthy seller must be able to complete a take, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS on the seller's ability to take any offer, freezing their ability to reduce debt? Proof idea: set up phantom bit for a reverting oracle, then call take and assert it reverts with oracle error.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy / withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower whose collateralBitmap is missing bit i (collateral[i]>0 but bit unset) withdraw a different collateral j (bit j set) that would make the position unhealthy if collateral[i] were not counted, under preconditions where the bitmap desync causes isHealthy to undercount maxDebt, via the sequence that creates desync → withdrawCollateral(j, amount) → isHealthy skips collateral[i] → maxDebt < debt → revert UnhealthyBorrower, violating the invariant that a borrower with sufficient total collateral must be able to withdraw collateral that leaves them healthy, causing scoped impact: fund freeze on legitimate collateral withdrawal? Proof idea: fuzz (collateral amounts, LLTV values) and assert withdrawCollateral succeeds when true aggregate collateral value supports health.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / isHealthy] Can an unprivileged liquidator liquidate a position that is actually healthy because collateralBitmap is missing bit i (collateral[i]>0 but bit unset), causing isHealthy and the liquidate loop to undercount maxDebt, under preconditions where the bitmap desync exists and originalDebt > computed maxDebt (but originalDebt <= true maxDebt), via the sequence that creates desync → liquidate(postMaturityMode=false) → maxDebt loop skips collateral[i] → originalDebt > maxDebt → NotLiquidatable check passes, violating the invariant that healthy accounts must not be liquidatable, causing scoped impact: unauthorized seizure of collateral from a healthy borrower? Proof idea: construct desync state, assert isHealthy returns true with correct bitmap but liquidate succeeds with corrupted bitmap.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral / countBits] Can an unprivileged borrower supply collateral to 16 distinct indices (hitting MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER), then withdraw all from one index (clearing that bit), then supply to a new index, and if countBits has an off-by-one or the bitmap has a phantom bit from a prior sequence, exceed MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER=16 without triggering TooManyActivatedCollaterals, under preconditions where the market has >=17 collateral slots, via the sequence supply×16 → withdraw(index_k, all) → supply(index_17), violating the invariant that countBits(newCollateralBitmap) <= MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER, causing scoped impact: bitmap with 17+ active bits causes isHealthy and liquidate loops to iterate more collaterals than expected, potentially overflowing maxDebt accumulation? Proof idea: fuzz supply/withdraw sequences and assert countBits(collateralBitmap) <= 16 at all times.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate (bad debt path)] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate with seizedAssets=0 and repaidUnits=0 on a collateralIndex that is not in the borrower's collateralBitmap, under preconditions where the position has nonzero debt and is unhealthy or post-maturity, via the sequence liquidate(collateralIndex=notInBitmap, seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=0), causing the loop to compute maxDebt and badDebt from the actual bitmap (excluding collateralIndex), then realize bad debt without touching the bitmap, violating the invariant that bad debt realization must account for all active collateral, causing scoped impact: bad debt over-realized if the non-activated collateral has value, slashing lenders incorrectly? [File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / msb] Can the msb() function (UtilsLib.sol:54-58, using the clz opcode) return an index i >= market.collateralParams.length when the collateralBitmap has a bit set at position >= N (number of collaterals in the market), under preconditions where a bitmap desync introduced a high bit (e.g., bit 127) in a market with only 2 collaterals, via the sequence that sets bit 127 → liquidate() → msb(bitmap) returns 127 → market.collateralParams[127] reverts with array out-of-bounds, violating the invariant that all set bits in collateralBitmap must correspond to valid collateralParams indices, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS on liquidation and isHealthy for the affected position? Proof idea: construct a state with bit >= collateralParams.length set, call liquidate and assert it reverts with array OOB.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral / setBit] Can an unprivileged borrower call supplyCollateral with collateralIndex >= 128 (if market.collateralParams.length could be 128 and index is 128), causing setBit(bitmap, 128) to silently no-op (since uint128(bitmap | (1 << 128)) truncates the high bit), under preconditions where the market has exactly 128 collaterals and collateralIndex=127 is the last valid index, via the sequence supplyCollateral(collateralIndex=127, assets>0) where oldCollateral=0, violating the invariant that setBit correctly sets bit 127 in a uint128, causing scoped impact: collateral[127] > 0 but bit 127 not set, making the highest-index collateral invisible to health checks? Proof idea: unit test setBit(0, 127) and assert result has bit 127 set; also test setBit(0, 128) and assert it is a no-op.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / clearBit] Can the liquidate function fail to clear bit i from collateralBitmap when seizedAssets > 0 but newCollateral > 0 (partial seizure), leaving the bit set correctly, but then a subsequent full seizure of the remaining collateral (seizedAssets = newCollateral) also fails to clear the bit if newCollateral - seizedAssets = 0 but the condition check is newCollateral == 0 && seizedAssets > 0, under preconditions where two sequential liquidations target the same collateralIndex, via the sequence liquidate(i, partial) → liquidate(i, remaining), violating the invariant that bit i is cleared when collateral[i] reaches zero, causing scoped impact: phantom bit for collateral[i]=0 causes oracle calls in subsequent health checks and liquidation loops, potentially causing DoS if oracle reverts? [File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy / take (SellerIsLiquidatable check)] Can an unprivileged taker cause a legitimate seller's take to revert by exploiting a bitmap desync where the seller's collateralBitmap is missing bit i (collateral[i]>0 but bit unset), making isHealthy undercount maxDebt and return false even though the seller is truly healthy, under preconditions where the seller has debt and the bitmap desync was introduced by a prior authorized operator action, via the sequence create desync → taker calls take(offer where seller=victim) → isHealthy(seller) returns false → revert SellerIsLiquidatable, violating the invariant that a healthy seller must be able to complete a take, causing scoped impact: DoS on the seller's ability to reduce debt via take, potentially trapping them in an undercollateralized state? [File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / ILiquidatorGate.canLiquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator bypass the ILiquidatorGate check by exploiting a bitmap desync that makes a healthy position appear unhealthy, under preconditions where the market has a liquidatorGate that allows the attacker but the position is actually healthy, via the sequence create bitmap desync (missing bit for high-value collateral) → liquidate() → canLiquidate(msg.sender) returns true → originalDebt > computed maxDebt (due to missing collateral) → liquidation proceeds, violating the invariant that only unhealthy positions are liquidatable before maturity, causing scoped impact: authorized liquidator seizes collateral from a healthy borrower, constituting unauthorized seizure? Proof idea: assert that for any bitmap desync, liquidate reverts NotLiquidatable when true health holds.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / RCF computation] Can a bitmap desync (missing bit for collateral j) cause the RCF (recovery close factor) computation at Midnight.sol:659-667 to use a wrong maxDebt value, allowing a liquidator to repay more than the RCF permits, under preconditions where the position is unhealthy and the bitmap is missing bit j (high-LLTV collateral), via the sequence create desync → liquidate(collateralIndex=i, repaidUnits=large) → maxDebt is undercomputed → (debt - maxDebt) is overestimated → maxRepaid is overestimated → RCF check passes for repaidUnits that should be capped, violating the invariant that repay amount is capped by what is needed to restore health, causing scoped impact: over-liquidation of a borrower, seizing more collateral than necessary? Proof idea: fuzz (collateral amounts, LLTV, bitmap state) and assert repaidUnits <= true RCF cap.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / badDebt computation] Can a bitmap desync (phantom bit for collateral k with collateral[k]=0) cause the badDebt computation loop at Midnight.sol:614-616 to call IOracle(collateralParam.oracle).price() for a zero-amount collateral, and if the oracle returns a nonzero price, compute a nonzero zeroFloorSub contribution that reduces badDebt below its true value, under preconditions where the phantom bit exists and the oracle for index k returns a high price, via the sequence create phantom bit → liquidate() → badDebt loop includes k → badDebt -= 0 * price / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE * WAD / maxLif = 0 (no effect since collateral[k]=0), violating the invariant that badDebt reflects only actual collateral value, causing scoped impact: no direct impact from zero-amount phantom, but oracle revert for phantom index causes liquidation DoS? Proof idea: unit test phantom bit with reverting oracle and assert liquidate reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: multicall / supplyCollateral / withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower use multicall to atomically supply collateral to index i (setting bit i), then withdraw all of index i (clearing bit i), then supply 1 wei to index i again (setting bit i), and repeat this pattern to manipulate the bitmap into a state where countBits is at the limit but a subsequent single supply to a new index j fails TooManyActivatedCollaterals, under preconditions where the borrower already has 15 active collaterals, via the sequence multicall([supply(i,X), withdraw(i,X), supply(i,1)]) → supply(j, Y) → TooManyActivatedCollaterals, violating the invariant that countBits(collateralBitmap) accurately reflects the number of active collaterals, causing scoped impact: DoS on adding a new collateral type? [File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / isHealthy (multi-collateral maxDebt)] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit a bitmap desync where bit i is set but collateral[i]=0 (phantom bit) for a collateral with a very high oracle price, causing the isHealthy loop to add 0 * price / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE * lltv / WAD = 0 to maxDebt (no effect), but the liquidate loop to also add 0 to maxDebt, meaning the phantom bit has no effect on maxDebt but does cause an extra oracle call, under preconditions where the phantom oracle is expensive or reverting, via the sequence create phantom bit → liquidate() → extra oracle call for phantom index, violating the invariant that the liquidation loop must not call oracles for zero-amount collaterals, causing scoped impact: gas griefing or DoS if phantom oracle reverts? Proof idea: fuzz phantom bit scenarios and assert no oracle call occurs for collateral[i]=0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral / authorization] Can an unprivileged attacker who is authorized for a victim borrower call supplyCollateral(market, collateralIndex, 0, victim) when victim.collateral[collateralIndex]=0, causing oldCollateral=0 and assets=0, so the setBit condition (oldCollateral==0 && assets>0) is false and no bit is set, but then call supplyCollateral(market, collateralIndex, X, victim) with X>0, causing oldCollateral=0 and assets=X>0, correctly setting the bit, under preconditions where the attacker is authorized, via the sequence authorize(attacker) → supplyCollateral(i, 0, victim) → supplyCollateral(i, X, victim), violating no invariant in this specific sequence (bit is correctly set on second call), but confirming that a zero-asset supply is a no-op for bitmap state? [File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / collateralBitmap local copy vs storage] Can the liquidate function's use of a local copy _collateralBitmap (Midnight.sol:606) for the maxDebt/badDebt loop, while only updating storage _position.collateralBitmap for the seized collateral (Midnight.sol:673), create a window where a reentrant onLiquidate callback reads the storage bitmap (already updated for seized collateral) but the loop has already finished computing maxDebt from the pre-seizure bitmap, under preconditions where the callback reads collateralBitmap(id, borrower) and uses it to make decisions, via the sequence liquidate() → seize collateral → clear bit in storage → onLiquidate callback reads updated bitmap → callback acts on stale maxDebt, violating the invariant that the bitmap state observed by the callback is consistent with the maxDebt/badDebt already computed? Proof idea: unit test with callback that reads collateralBitmap and assert it matches the state used for maxDebt computation.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy / take (seller health check)] Can an unprivileged maker (seller) whose collateralBitmap has bit i set for a collateral with a very high oracle price, but collateral[i]=0 (phantom bit), cause isHealthy to compute maxDebt = 0 * price / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE * lltv / WAD = 0 for that slot, meaning the phantom bit contributes nothing to maxDebt, so if the seller's debt > sum of real collateral values, isHealthy returns false and the take reverts SellerIsLiquidatable, under preconditions where the phantom bit exists and the seller's real collateral barely covers debt, via the sequence create phantom bit → take(offer where seller=maker) → isHealthy returns false → revert, violating the invariant that a seller with sufficient real collateral must be able to complete a take? [File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / post-maturity mode] Can an unprivileged liquidator in post-maturity mode exploit a bitmap desync (missing bit for collateral i) to liquidate a position where the true maxDebt (including collateral[i]) would make the position healthy pre-maturity, but the computed maxDebt (excluding collateral[i]) makes it appear unhealthy, under preconditions where block.timestamp > market.maturity and the bitmap desync exists, via the sequence create desync → liquidate(postMaturityMode=true) → NotLiquidatable check: postMaturityMode=true so only block.timestamp > market.maturity is checked → liquidation proceeds regardless of health, violating the invariant that post-maturity liquidation is valid regardless of health (this is by design), but causing scoped impact: the badDebt computation undercounts collateral[i], over-realizing bad debt and over-slashing lenders? Proof idea: assert badDebt in post-maturity liquidation equals debt minus sum of all collateral values including non-bitmap collateral.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / maxDebt loop (Midnight.sol:607-618)] Can the while loop in liquidate that iterates _collateralBitmap using msb() and clearBit() skip a collateral if msb() returns an index i where market.collateralParams[i].lltv=0 (hypothetically), causing mulDivDown(collateral, price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivDown(0, WAD)=0 contribution to maxDebt, under preconditions where a collateral slot has lltv=0 (not possible given isLltvAllowed, but fuzz boundary), via the sequence touchMarket with lltv=LLTV_0=0.385e18 (minimum) → supply collateral → check maxDebt contribution, violating the invariant that maxDebt accurately reflects all active collateral at their respective LLTVs? Proof idea: fuzz all allowed LLTV values and assert maxDebt = sum(collateral[i] * price[i] / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE * lltv[i] / WAD) for all set bits.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral / withdrawCollateral (bitmap sync invariant)] Can a stateful fuzz test over arbitrary sequences of supplyCollateral, withdrawCollateral, and liquidate calls find any state where collateralBitmap bit i is set but collateral[i]=0, or bit i is unset but collateral[i]>0, under preconditions of arbitrary valid market parameters and arbitrary authorized callers, via a stateful invariant test that after every state-changing call asserts for all i in [0, market.collateralParams.length): (collateralBitmap >> i) & 1 == (collateral[i] > 0 ? 1 : 0), violating the core bitmap sync invariant, causing scoped impact: any desync enables health check manipulation or oracle DoS?
